unresolved.text: Intestinal malrotation with volvulus, which is due to incomplete rotation of the intestine in utero, is estimated to have a prevalence of between 1 in 200 and 1 in 6000 live births, with 75% of cases occurring during the first year of life, and the escalating frequency and severity of this patient’s episodic, acute-onset abdominal pain and vomiting raise the possibility of an intermittent process originating from a congenital disorder that might require surgical intervention.
The pattern of intermittent, recurrent episodes of upper abdominal pain and vomiting in this patient is similar to that seen in abdominal migraine.

Intestinal malrotation with volvulus is a rare congenital disorder that can cause abdominal pain and vomiting, and the escalating frequency and severity of this patient’s episodic, acute-onset abdominal pain and vomiting raise the possibility of an intermittent process originating from a congenital disorder that might require surgical intervention.

I recall similar cases of abdominal pain in children, where the diagnosis was not immediately apparent, and a thorough evaluation, including imaging studies and surgical consultation, was necessary to determine the underlying cause.
